dimensions.d: The extent to which the statement establishes mechanisms for holding AI developers, deployers, or operators responsible for harms or failures. Strong coverage includes: specifies liability frameworks, audit requirements, or accountability chains; names responsible parties; mandates post-deployment monitoring or incident reporting obligations.

The extent to which the statement protects human agency, self-determination, and independent decision-making from erosion by AI systems. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses AI-driven manipulation, nudging, or choice architecture; mandates protections against autonomy-undermining personalization; prohibits subliminal or coercive AI influence.

The extent to which the statement orients AI development toward human flourishing, the common good, or broad societal benefit. Strong coverage includes: centers AI development around measurable social outcomes (health, education, poverty reduction); mandates public benefit assessments; explicitly subordinates commercial interests to public good.

The extent to which the statement establishes rights to challenge AI decisions and obtain meaningful remedy for harms. Strong coverage includes: mandates appeal mechanisms for automated decisions; specifies timelines and procedures for contestation; requires human review upon challenge; establishes compensation or restitution frameworks.

The extent to which the statement demands that advanced AI systems remain correctable, interruptible, and redirectable by human operators. Strong coverage includes: explicitly requires kill switches, shutdown mechanisms, or alignment guarantees; mandates that AI systems cannot resist correction or develop self-preservation goals; references corrigibility by name.

The extent to which the statement asserts control over personal, community, or national data, including data localization requirements and resistance to cross-border data extraction. Strong coverage includes: demands data localization, community data trusts, or indigenous data sovereignty (e.g., CARE principles); opposes cross-border data extraction without consent; specifies data governance architectures.

The extent to which the statement addresses prevention of AI misuse for harmful purposes, including weaponization, surveillance, and disinformation. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific dual-use risks (bioweapon design, autonomous targeting, mass surveillance); mandates use restrictions, export controls, or know-your-customer requirements for AI capabilities.

The extent to which the statement addresses the environmental footprint of AI systems, including energy consumption, carbon emissions, and resource extraction. Strong coverage includes: mandates carbon accounting for AI training and inference; requires energy efficiency standards or renewable energy use for data centers; addresses water use, mineral extraction, or e-waste from AI hardware.

The extent to which the statement demands truthful, non-misleading communication about AI capabilities, limitations, uncertainties, and known failure modes. Strong coverage includes: explicitly prohibits overstatement of AI capabilities; requires disclosure of known limitations, error rates, or confidence intervals; mandates honesty about what AI cannot do.

The extent to which the statement addresses non-discrimination, bias mitigation, and equitable treatment in AI systems. Strong coverage includes: mandates bias audits, fairness metrics, or disparate impact assessments; specifies protected categories; requires pre-deployment testing for discriminatory outcomes.

The extent to which the statement invokes respect for inherent human worth, resisting the reduction of persons to data points or optimization targets. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes human dignity as a foundational constraint on AI; prohibits AI uses that degrade, dehumanize, or instrumentalize persons; references dignity in constitutional or human rights frameworks.

The extent to which the statement requires human-in-the-loop or human-on-the-loop oversight for AI operations, particularly in high-stakes decisions. Strong coverage includes: mandates human review before consequential AI decisions (sentencing, hiring, medical diagnosis); specifies human-in-the-loop or human-on-the-loop requirements; defines domains where full automation is prohibited.

The extent to which the statement ensures AI benefits, tools, and governance processes are accessible to all populations including marginalized and underrepresented groups. Strong coverage includes: mandates accessibility standards (WCAG) for AI interfaces; requires multilingual support; addresses digital divides; mandates inclusive design processes involving marginalized communities.

The extent to which the statement requires meaningful, informed consent from individuals or communities before AI data collection, processing, or automated decision-making. Strong coverage includes: mandates explicit, informed, and revocable consent for data use in AI training or deployment; specifies consent must be granular, purpose-specific, and freely given; addresses power asymmetries in consent.

The extent to which the statement addresses data protection, privacy preservation, and limits on data collection in AI systems. Strong coverage includes: invokes specific data protection frameworks (GDPR, CCPA); mandates privacy-by-design, data minimization, or differential privacy; requires data protection impact assessments.

The extent to which the statement restricts AI systems and data use to their stated purposes, opposing function creep or secondary repurposing. Strong coverage includes: explicitly prohibits repurposing of data or AI systems beyond original stated purpose; mandates purpose specification and technical enforcement of use restrictions; addresses function creep.

The extent to which the statement demands that AI systems perform reliably, resist adversarial attacks, and degrade gracefully under unexpected conditions. Strong coverage includes: mandates adversarial robustness testing, formal verification, or reliability certification; specifies performance standards under distribution shift; requires fail-safe or graceful degradation mechanisms.

The extent to which the statement addresses prevention of large-scale, civilizational, or existential risks from advanced AI systems. Strong coverage includes: explicitly references existential risk, loss of human control over superintelligent AI, or civilizational-scale catastrophe; calls for moratoriums, international treaties, or compute governance to prevent catastrophic outcomes.

The extent to which the statement addresses prevention of concrete, immediate, and foreseeable harms from current AI systems. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific near-term harms (deepfakes, autonomous weapons misuse, algorithmic discrimination); mandates safety testing, red-teaming, or pre-deployment risk assessments; specifies harm thresholds.

The extent to which the statement addresses protection of AI systems from cyberattacks, data breaches, model theft, and adversarial exploitation. Strong coverage includes: mandates cybersecurity standards for AI systems; addresses model poisoning, prompt injection, or data exfiltration; requires security audits or certification; references specific threat models.

The extent to which the statement calls for AI systems to be transparent about their workings, decision logic, and outputs. Strong coverage includes: explicitly demands algorithmic transparency or explainability; invokes right to explanation; mandates disclosure requirements for model architecture, training data, or decision criteria.

The extent to which the statement addresses children's best interests and protection from AI harms, including data collection, profiling, and age-inappropriate content. Strong coverage includes: invokes CRC (Convention on the Rights of the Child); mandates age-appropriate design, prohibits behavioral profiling of children, addresses AI in education and child welfare; requires child impact assessments.

The extent to which the statement addresses disability rights and reasonable accommodation in AI design, deployment, and governance. Strong coverage includes: invokes CRPD; mandates accessibility-by-design in AI systems; addresses AI discrimination against disabled persons (hiring algorithms, insurance, benefits); requires assistive AI development.

The extent to which the statement demands equal protection under law in algorithmic decision-making, particularly in criminal justice, welfare, immigration, and public services. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific domains (criminal sentencing, welfare eligibility, immigration screening) where algorithmic decisions must meet equal-protection standards; mandates disparate-impact analysis; requires judicial or administrative review.

The extent to which the statement addresses freedom of speech and expression in the context of algorithmic content moderation, recommendation systems, and AI-generated content. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses algorithmic censorship, content moderation bias, or chilling effects of AI surveillance on speech; mandates transparency in content moderation algorithms; opposes AI-enabled suppression of dissent.

The extent to which the statement addresses religious freedom in AI-mediated contexts, including algorithmic profiling based on religion and AI's impact on religious practice. Strong coverage includes: explicitly prohibits AI-based religious profiling or surveillance of religious communities; addresses AI content moderation of religious expression; considers religious accommodation in AI systems.

The extent to which the statement protects cognitive liberty, mental privacy, and freedom from AI-driven manipulation of beliefs, preferences, or emotions. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes cognitive liberty or mental privacy; opposes neuro-surveillance, emotion recognition for manipulation, or AI systems designed to alter beliefs without awareness; references brain-computer interface governance.

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to request a human decision-maker instead of an AI system. Strong coverage includes: explicitly mandates the right to a human alternative; requires human decision-makers to be available upon request; specifies contexts where human alternatives are mandatory (healthcare, justice, employment).

The extent to which the statement addresses indigenous peoples' rights to self-determination, cultural preservation, and data sovereignty in AI contexts. Strong coverage includes: invokes UNDRIP; addresses indigenous data sovereignty (OCAP, CARE principles); mandates free, prior, and informed consent for AI affecting indigenous communities; opposes extractive data practices on indigenous knowledge.

The extent to which the statement addresses protection of racial, ethnic, linguistic, and other minority groups from AI harms and ensures their representation in AI governance. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific minority groups facing AI harms (racial profiling, linguistic exclusion, cultural erasure); mandates disaggregated impact data; requires minority representation in AI governance bodies.

The extent to which the statement invokes non-discrimination and equality before the law as human rights principles constraining AI systems. Strong coverage includes: invokes ICERD, CEDAW, CRPD, or other anti-discrimination treaties; identifies specific AI discrimination vectors (race, gender, disability, age); mandates algorithmic impact assessments on protected groups.

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to challenge and override automated decisions, including the right to opt out of algorithmic processing. Strong coverage includes: mandates the right to human review of automated decisions; establishes opt-out rights from algorithmic processing; specifies appeal procedures and timelines; requires that contested decisions be suspended pending review.

The extent to which the statement addresses the right to participate in cultural life in the digital/AI age, including protection of cultural heritage, linguistic diversity, and creative expression. Strong coverage includes: addresses AI's impact on cultural diversity, linguistic preservation, or creative expression; opposes cultural homogenization through AI; mandates multilingual AI development; protects cultural heritage from AI extraction.

The extent to which the statement addresses the right to education, including digital literacy, AI literacy, and equitable access to AI-enhanced learning. Strong coverage includes: mandates AI literacy curricula in public education; demands equitable access to AI learning tools; addresses risks of AI-driven educational sorting or tracking; invokes right to education (ICESCR Art. 13).

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to receive a meaningful explanation after an automated decision has been made. Strong coverage includes: mandates individualized, understandable explanations of automated decisions; specifies what constitutes a meaningful explanation (factors, weights, alternatives); requires explanations in plain language; sets response timelines.

The extent to which the statement addresses the right to health in AI contexts, including AI in medical diagnosis, drug discovery, health data, and equitable healthcare access. Strong coverage includes: invokes right to health (ICESCR Art. 12); addresses AI diagnostic bias in healthcare, health data governance, or equitable access to AI-enhanced medicine; mandates clinical validation and regulatory approval for AI health tools.

The extent to which the statement addresses the right to housing in AI contexts, including algorithmic tenant screening, dynamic pricing, and AI-driven gentrification. Strong coverage includes: identifies algorithmic tenant screening, rent-setting algorithms, or AI-driven housing discrimination as rights concerns; mandates fair housing audits for AI systems; addresses automated eviction decisions.

The extent to which the statement addresses protection of life and physical security in AI contexts, including lethal autonomous weapons and AI in critical infrastructure. Strong coverage includes: explicitly opposes lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS); invokes IHRL Article 6 (right to life); demands human authorization for any AI system with lethal capability; addresses AI in critical safety systems.

The extent to which the statement protects democratic participation from AI manipulation, including micro-targeting, deepfakes in elections, and algorithmic suppression of political speech. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses AI-driven political micro-targeting, deepfake threats to elections, or algorithmic voter suppression; mandates transparency in political AI use; calls for regulation of AI in campaigns.

The extent to which the statement frames privacy as a fundamental human right rather than merely a data-protection compliance matter, invoking bodily autonomy, spatial privacy, and informational self-determination. Strong coverage includes: invokes privacy as an inalienable human right (UDHR Art. 12, ICCPR Art. 17); addresses mass surveillance, biometric identification in public spaces, or predictive policing as human rights violations; demands moratoriums on invasive AI surveillance.

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to opt out of AI-mediated processes entirely. Strong coverage includes: explicitly establishes the right to refuse AI interaction; mandates non-AI alternatives in essential services; prohibits compulsory AI-mediated processes without consent.

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to effective legal remedy for harms caused by AI systems, including access to courts, compensation, and systemic relief. Strong coverage includes: mandates accessible judicial or quasi-judicial remedies for AI harms; specifies burden-of-proof allocation (shifting to deployer); addresses collective redress and class actions; establishes compensation frameworks.

The extent to which the statement addresses social safety nets and welfare protections in the context of AI-driven economic disruption. Strong coverage includes: demands strengthened social protection systems to address AI displacement; proposes specific mechanisms (UBI, portable benefits, social insurance reform); invokes ICESCR Art. 9 (right to social security).

The extent to which the statement establishes the right to be informed when AI is being used in decisions affecting an individual. Strong coverage includes: mandates disclosure whenever AI is used in consequential decisions; requires labeling of AI-generated content; specifies timing and format of notice; addresses covert AI use as a rights violation.

The extent to which the statement addresses labor rights and the right to work in the context of AI-driven automation and workplace transformation. Strong coverage includes: invokes right to work (ICESCR Art. 6); demands impact assessments before AI-driven workforce displacement; mandates transition support, retraining, or new employment guarantees; addresses gig-economy labor rights.

The extent to which the statement addresses corruption risks in AI procurement, governance, and the revolving door between tech companies and regulators. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific corruption vectors (AI procurement fraud, revolving door, regulatory capture, lobbying); mandates conflict-of-interest disclosures, cooling-off periods, or procurement integrity standards.

The extent to which the statement demands constitutional or institutional checks on concentrations of AI-related power, whether governmental or corporate. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific concentrations of AI power (big tech, intelligence agencies, executive authority) and demands structural countervailing mechanisms; proposes new institutional checks for the AI era.

The extent to which the statement demands meaningful roles for civil society organizations, NGOs, and community groups in AI governance. Strong coverage includes: mandates civil society seats on AI governance bodies; requires public comment periods for AI regulations; funds civil society capacity for AI oversight; establishes participatory governance structures.

The extent to which the statement demands democratic control, legitimacy, and popular mandate for AI policy decisions. Strong coverage includes: demands that AI policy be subject to democratic processes (legislative debate, referenda, citizens' assemblies); opposes technocratic or corporate capture of AI governance; requires elected-body oversight.

The extent to which the statement addresses protection of elections from AI-enabled manipulation, including deepfakes, micro-targeting, and computational propaganda. Strong coverage includes: demands regulation of AI in political campaigns; mandates labeling of AI-generated political content; proposes restrictions on algorithmic micro-targeting during elections; addresses foreign AI-enabled election interference.

The extent to which the statement addresses press freedom in covering AI issues, including access to information, whistleblower protection, and resistance to AI-enabled media manipulation. Strong coverage includes: demands press access to AI system information for investigative journalism; protects whistleblowers who expose AI harms; addresses AI-generated disinformation as a threat to press integrity.

The extent to which the statement demands that governments be accountable for their own AI deployments in public services, law enforcement, and administration. Strong coverage includes: mandates government AI registries, public audits of government AI systems, or algorithmic impact assessments for public-sector AI; requires legislative reporting on government AI use.

The extent to which the statement calls for independent regulatory agencies, ombudspersons, or audit bodies for AI oversight. Strong coverage includes: calls for independent AI regulatory agencies with statutory authority, funding, and enforcement power; specifies independence criteria (budget autonomy, appointment procedures); references existing models (data protection authorities).

The extent to which the statement emphasizes public understanding of AI capabilities, risks, and governance as essential for democratic self-governance. Strong coverage includes: mandates public AI literacy programs; funds independent AI journalism and research; requires plain-language explanations of AI policies; addresses AI misinformation as a democratic threat.

The extent to which the statement addresses the legitimacy conditions for AI governance institutions, including representativeness, procedural fairness, and accountability. Strong coverage includes: explicitly analyzes legitimacy deficits in current AI governance (unrepresentative, unaccountable, dominated by Global North or industry); proposes legitimacy criteria for AI governance bodies.

The extent to which the statement frames liberal democratic values (individual rights, pluralism, constitutionalism) as the normative foundation for AI governance. Strong coverage includes: explicitly positions liberal democratic values as the governing framework for AI; opposes authoritarian AI governance models; frames AI governance as an extension of constitutional democracy.

The extent to which the statement demands transparency and openness in government AI decisions, procurement, and policy-making. Strong coverage includes: mandates open-source government AI systems, public algorithmic impact assessments, or FOIA applicability to government AI; requires public registers of government AI use cases.

The extent to which the statement calls for structured public deliberation on AI policy, including citizens' assemblies, public consultations, and deliberative processes. Strong coverage includes: calls for citizens' assemblies, deliberative polls, or structured public consultations on AI policy; references specific deliberative methods; mandates public input before major AI deployments.

The extent to which the statement demands that AI governance be subject to legal frameworks, statutory authority, and judicial review rather than industry self-regulation alone. Strong coverage includes: explicitly calls for binding AI legislation, statutory regulatory authority, or treaty-based governance; opposes purely voluntary codes; demands judicial reviewability of AI-related decisions.

The extent to which the statement addresses institutional checks on AI governance power, preventing concentration of AI authority in any single branch or entity. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses separation of powers in AI governance; demands that regulatory, enforcement, and adjudicative functions be distributed; opposes executive overreach in AI policy; requires legislative authorization.

The extent to which the statement advocates for AI governance decisions to be made at the most local appropriate level, resisting unnecessary centralization. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes subsidiarity; demands local or regional AI governance authority; opposes top-down AI policy that ignores local contexts; advocates for community-level AI governance.

The extent to which the statement prioritizes workforce development, AI training programs, and building AI human capital as a strategic priority. Strong coverage includes: central focus on AI workforce development; proposes national AI training programs, university curricula, or reskilling initiatives as strategic priorities; sets targets for AI talent pipeline.

The extent to which the statement addresses the financialization of AI, including speculative investment, valuation bubbles, and the distortion of AI development by financial incentives. Strong coverage includes: critiques speculative AI investment, hype cycles, or misaligned financial incentives driving unsafe AI development; proposes guardrails on AI-related financial speculation; addresses venture capital influence on AI safety trade-offs.

The extent to which the statement frames AI primarily as a tool for achieving national development goals, rather than as an object of governance. Strong coverage includes: frames AI predominantly as an instrument for economic development, poverty reduction, or SDG achievement; subordinates governance concerns to developmental outcomes; treats AI adoption as development strategy.

The extent to which the statement addresses AI-driven workplace surveillance, performance monitoring, scheduling, and management of workers. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific algorithmic management harms (constant surveillance, automated firing, pace-setting, emotional monitoring); demands transparency in workplace algorithms; mandates worker access to their own performance data.

The extent to which the statement opposes monopolistic concentration of AI capabilities, data, compute, or market power in a small number of corporations. Strong coverage includes: names specific concentration risks (Big Tech dominance, compute moats, data monopolies); demands structural separation, acquisition limits, or mandatory licensing; proposes public alternatives to concentrated private AI.

The extent to which the statement supports worker collective bargaining rights, unionization, and worker voice in AI-related workplace decisions. Strong coverage includes: mandates collective bargaining over AI deployment in workplaces; supports worker right to organize around AI issues; requires worker consent before algorithmic management implementation; references ILO conventions.

The extent to which the statement critiques data rent-seeking, data enclosure, and the appropriation of collectively-produced data by private actors. Strong coverage includes: explicitly critiques data enclosure or data rent-seeking; proposes data commons, data trusts, or data dividends; frames personal/community data as a commons rather than a corporate asset; references Zuboff, Morozov, or political economy of data.

The extent to which the statement addresses access to the digital infrastructure (broadband, compute, cloud, devices) necessary to participate in the AI economy. Strong coverage includes: demands universal broadband, public compute infrastructure, or subsidized access to cloud/AI services; frames digital infrastructure as essential public utility; addresses infrastructure gaps between urban/rural, rich/poor, North/South.

The extent to which the statement proposes economic safety nets, universal basic income, or other income guarantees in response to AI-driven economic disruption. Strong coverage includes: proposes UBI, universal basic services, or other unconditional income guarantees funded by AI-related revenues; frames economic security as a right in the AI era; specifies funding mechanisms.

The extent to which the statement demands fair distribution of AI-generated wealth, productivity gains, and social benefits. Strong coverage includes: proposes specific redistribution mechanisms (profit-sharing, public AI dividends, community benefit agreements); demands that productivity gains be shared with workers and communities; frames AI benefits as a commons.

The extent to which the statement addresses taxation of AI profits, automation, or data extraction to fund public goods. Strong coverage includes: proposes specific AI-related taxes (robot tax, automation tax, data tax, windfall profits tax on AI companies); addresses tax avoidance by AI corporations; links AI taxation to funding public services.

The extent to which the statement addresses financial inclusion and exclusion through AI, including algorithmic credit scoring, insurance pricing, and banking access. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific financial exclusion risks (algorithmic credit denial, discriminatory insurance pricing, automated loan rejection); mandates fairness audits in financial AI; requires alternative pathways for AI-excluded individuals.

The extent to which the statement addresses AI's impact on global supply chains, manufacturing, and the international division of labor. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI's reshoring effects, displacement of Global South manufacturing labor, or restructuring of global value chains; addresses content moderation labor in the Global South; proposes transition mechanisms for affected economies.

The extent to which the statement emphasizes AI as a driver of innovation, productivity, and economic growth. Strong coverage includes: centers AI policy around enabling innovation and competitiveness; opposes regulation that might slow AI development; frames AI governance primarily in terms of economic opportunity; invokes national competitive advantage.

The extent to which the statement addresses IP rights in AI contexts, including training data rights, AI-generated output ownership, and patent implications. Strong coverage includes: takes a specific position on AI training data copyright, AI-generated content ownership, or AI patent eligibility; proposes new IP frameworks for the AI era; addresses creators' rights vis-a-vis AI training.

The extent to which the statement addresses worker retraining, transition support, and workforce development in response to AI-driven automation. Strong coverage includes: mandates employer-funded retraining programs; proposes sector-specific transition plans; establishes advance-notice requirements for AI-driven layoffs; funds public workforce development for AI-displaced workers.

The extent to which the statement addresses competitive dynamics in AI markets, including barriers to entry, interoperability, and market concentration. Strong coverage includes: demands antitrust enforcement in AI markets; mandates interoperability or data portability; identifies specific market concentration concerns (compute, data, foundation models); proposes structural remedies.

The extent to which the statement addresses technology gaps, dependencies, and power asymmetries between Global North and Global South in AI development and governance. Strong coverage includes: identifies structural North-South AI asymmetries (compute concentration, data extraction, brain drain, standard-setting dominance); proposes technology transfer, capacity building, or South-led AI governance; references digital colonialism.

The extent to which the statement advocates for open-source AI development, public AI infrastructure, or commons-based approaches to AI. Strong coverage includes: demands public investment in open-source AI models; proposes national or public AI compute infrastructure; frames AI as a public good requiring commons-based governance; opposes proprietary lock-in of foundational AI capabilities.

The extent to which the statement addresses platform monopoly rent extraction, including app store fees, API pricing, and intermediary power in AI ecosystems. Strong coverage includes: identifies specific platform rent-extraction mechanisms (app store commissions, API paywalls, data lock-in); proposes interoperability mandates, platform utility regulation, or platform cooperativism; references platform capitalism critique.

The extent to which the statement addresses fairness, transparency, and accountability in government and institutional AI procurement. Strong coverage includes: mandates open, competitive AI procurement processes; requires algorithmic impact assessments before procurement; demands vendor transparency and accountability; prohibits sole-source AI contracts above thresholds.

The extent to which the statement addresses small and medium enterprise access to AI tools, markets, and infrastructure. Strong coverage includes: mandates SME access programs for AI; proposes public AI infrastructure or shared compute for small businesses; addresses AI vendor lock-in; requires AI-literate business support services.

The extent to which the statement critiques the economic logic of surveillance capitalism — the extraction and commodification of behavioral data for prediction and profit. Strong coverage includes: explicitly references surveillance capitalism (Zuboff) or equivalent critique; identifies behavioral surplus extraction as a structural economic logic; proposes structural alternatives (ban behavioral advertising, data minimization mandates, public alternatives).

The extent to which the statement addresses the use of AI technology access as a condition in aid, trade, or diplomatic relationships. Strong coverage includes: identifies AI technology conditionality in trade agreements, development aid, or diplomatic relations; critiques or endorses technology transfer restrictions (e.g., chip export controls); addresses AI governance requirements as trade conditions.

The extent to which the statement addresses building domestic AI production capacity versus consuming imported AI technology. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses technology transfer mechanisms; proposes building domestic AI chip fabrication, model training infrastructure, or national AI platforms; frames local production capacity as strategic priority.

The extent to which the statement addresses AI disruption of artistic creation, authorship, and copyright in creative industries. Strong coverage includes: centrally addresses AI-generated art, music, or text; analyzes copyright implications, authorship attribution, or economic impact on creative workers; proposes legal or governance frameworks for AI creativity.

Combating AI-generated or AI-amplified misinformation, deepfakes, and epistemic manipulation. Strong coverage includes: focuses on AI-driven disinformation risks; proposes detection mechanisms, provenance standards, or platform governance interventions against synthetic media.

Protection of bodily autonomy and reproductive rights in AI-mediated contexts (surveillance, health AI, predictive systems). Strong coverage includes: directly analyzes AI threats to bodily autonomy or reproductive rights; addresses fertility tracking surveillance, algorithmic pregnancy discrimination, or reproductive health AI bias.

Right of communities to self-govern over local AI deployment and data use. Strong coverage includes: argues for community veto or consent mechanisms over local AI deployment; proposes community data trusts, local governance boards, or participatory technology assessment.

Commitment to cultural and value pluralism in AI design, deployment, and governance. Strong coverage includes: central argument for value pluralism in AI; critiques monocultural AI norms; proposes participatory design across cultural contexts or pluralistic value alignment methods.

Protection of cultural heritage, traditions, and local knowledge systems from AI-driven homogenization. Strong coverage includes: explicitly argues AI risks cultural erasure or homogenization; proposes mechanisms for heritage protection, culturally-grounded datasets, or local knowledge safeguards.

The extent to which the statement addresses protecting cultural, linguistic, or religious identity from AI-driven homogenization. Strong coverage includes: explicitly argues AI risks cultural or religious identity erosion; proposes mechanisms for protecting cultural values, local languages, or traditional practices from AI-driven homogenization; addresses algorithmic monoculture.

Protection of family bonds, caregiving relationships, and intimate social structures from AI disruption. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI impacts on family dynamics, caregiving, or intimate relationships in depth; proposes safeguards for relational autonomy in domestic/care contexts.

Fairness across generations in AI policy, including obligations to future generations and protection of children/youth. Strong coverage includes: explicitly frames AI governance as intergenerational issue; discusses long-term impacts on future generations or age-differentiated risks; proposes intergenerational impact assessments.

Equitable access to AI knowledge, open science principles, and democratization of AI research. Strong coverage includes: advocates open-source AI, open datasets, or open science mandates; critiques proprietary knowledge hoarding; proposes knowledge commons for AI.

Linguistic diversity in AI systems and equitable access to language technologies across languages. Strong coverage includes: centrally addresses NLP resource gaps, language endangerment through AI, or proposes multilingual AI equity frameworks; discusses low-resource language inclusion strategies.

Concern for mental health impacts of AI systems, including addictive design, surveillance anxiety, and algorithmic harm to psychological wellbeing. Strong coverage includes: directly investigates AI mental health impacts (addictive UX, content harms, surveillance stress); proposes wellbeing metrics, design standards, or therapeutic safeguards.

Protection of religious freedom, conscience, and faith expression in AI-mediated contexts. Strong coverage includes: directly addresses AI impacts on religious practice, conscience rights, or faith-based accommodation; proposes protections for religious expression in algorithmic systems.

Concern for maintaining social bonds and community solidarity in AI-mediated contexts. Strong coverage includes: central theme; argues AI threatens or must strengthen social bonds; proposes social impact assessments or community-level interventions.

AI effects on social mobility, class stratification, and economic opportunity structures. Strong coverage includes: centrally analyzes how AI reshapes class structures, opportunity ladders, or meritocratic pathways; proposes policies to prevent AI-driven stratification.

AI's role in enabling or combating gender-based violence, including deepfake abuse, stalkerware, and online harassment. Strong coverage includes: centrally addresses technology-facilitated GBV; analyzes deepfake intimate imagery, AI stalkerware, algorithmic harassment amplification; proposes detection or legal remedies.

Energy consumption and carbon emissions from AI training, inference, and infrastructure. Strong coverage includes: quantifies or critically analyzes AI energy costs; discusses training compute emissions, data center impacts, or proposes carbon budgets for AI development.

Use of AI as a tool for environmental monitoring, remote sensing, and ecological assessment. Strong coverage includes: details AI applications in environmental monitoring (satellite imagery analysis, sensor networks, pollution tracking); evaluates effectiveness or proposes deployment frameworks.

AI's role in protecting or threatening biodiversity and ecosystem health. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI applications for biodiversity monitoring, species protection, or habitat conservation; or critiques AI infrastructure impacts on ecosystems.

AI's role in climate change mitigation, including climate modeling, emissions reduction, and clean energy optimization. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI as tool for climate mitigation in depth; discusses climate modeling, grid optimization, or emissions tracking; proposes AI-climate policy integration.

Intersection of environmental harms and social equity in AI deployment, including disproportionate impacts on marginalized communities. Strong coverage includes: analyzes how AI environmental costs (data centers, e-waste, mining) disproportionately burden marginalized communities; proposes environmental justice frameworks for AI siting and supply chains.

Standards and benchmarks for energy-efficient AI development, training, and deployment. Strong coverage includes: proposes or evaluates specific green AI standards; discusses compute-efficiency metrics, energy reporting mandates, or model compression for sustainability.

Recognition of ecosystem or nature rights in AI governance, including legal personhood for natural entities. Strong coverage includes: invokes rights-of-nature frameworks for AI governance; proposes legal standing for ecosystems in AI impact decisions or nature-centered AI design principles.

AI governance situated within planetary boundaries framework, respecting Earth system limits. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes planetary boundaries or Earth system limits as constraints on AI development; proposes governance aligned with biophysical thresholds.

Sustainable resource use in AI hardware supply chains, including rare earth minerals, water, and materials. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI hardware supply chain sustainability; discusses rare earth extraction, water consumption for cooling, or proposes sustainable sourcing standards.

The extent to which the statement asserts national sovereignty over AI models, training data, and computational infrastructure. Strong coverage includes: explicitly asserts AI sovereignty; proposes national control over AI training data, model development, or compute infrastructure; frames data localization or model sovereignty as national security priorities.

Right of colonized or marginalized communities to control algorithms that govern their lives. Strong coverage includes: argues for community control over algorithmic systems affecting them; proposes algorithmic impact assessments led by affected communities or indigenous data governance protocols.

Critique of Eurocentric or Western-centric norms in AI ethics and governance frameworks. Strong coverage includes: explicitly critiques Eurocentrism in AI ethics; proposes alternative normative foundations from non-Western traditions; deconstructs universalist claims as culturally specific.

Arms control and nonproliferation frameworks applied to AI capabilities. Strong coverage includes: proposes AI arms control regimes, nonproliferation treaties, or export controls on AI capabilities; analyzes AI arms race dynamics with specific control mechanisms.

Authoritarian framing of AI governance emphasizing stability, performance legitimacy, social order, or state-directed innovation. Strong coverage includes: articulates or analyzes authoritarian AI governance rationale (social stability, performance legitimacy, state-led development); engages with non-liberal governance models substantively.

Critique of data extraction from the Global South and marginalized communities by powerful actors. Strong coverage includes: explicitly uses data colonialism framework; analyzes extractive data practices from Global South or indigenous communities; proposes data sovereignty or anti-extractive governance.

Equitable AI development for developing nations, including technology transfer, capacity building, and bridging the AI divide. Strong coverage includes: centrally analyzes the AI divide between Global North and South; proposes technology transfer mechanisms, capacity building, or equitable development frameworks.

National or regional control over digital infrastructure, data flows, and platform governance. Strong coverage includes: analyzes digital sovereignty strategies (data localization, sovereign cloud, platform regulation); proposes frameworks for national/regional control over digital infrastructure.

Right of peoples and nations to produce knowledge on their own terms, resisting epistemic dependence on dominant AI paradigms. Strong coverage includes: invokes epistemic sovereignty or postcolonial knowledge politics; critiques dependence on Western AI knowledge production; proposes indigenous or local epistemic frameworks for AI.

Framing of AI, data, or foundational models as global commons requiring shared governance. Strong coverage includes: explicitly argues AI or data should be governed as global commons; proposes shared governance structures, commons licensing, or anti-enclosure mechanisms.

Human security paradigm (broader than state security) applied to AI governance, encompassing food, health, economic, environmental, personal, community, and political security. Strong coverage includes: invokes human security framework explicitly; analyzes AI impacts across multiple security dimensions (food, health, economic, personal); proposes people-centered AI security governance.

International cooperation, multilateral governance, and harmonization of AI standards across nations. Strong coverage includes: advocates multilateral AI governance frameworks; analyzes international treaty mechanisms, harmonization efforts, or proposes new international AI institutions.

Governance of military AI, including lethal autonomous weapons systems (LAWS), autonomous targeting, and military AI ethics. Strong coverage includes: focuses on LAWS governance, autonomous weapons bans, or military AI ethics; analyzes meaningful human control, CCW processes, or military AI accountability.

Multi-polar world order in AI governance, resisting unipolar dominance by any single power. Strong coverage includes: advocates multi-polar AI governance architecture; analyzes US-China duopoly risks; proposes governance structures ensuring diverse power centers in AI standard-setting.

National sovereignty over AI governance, including the right of states to regulate AI within their jurisdiction. Strong coverage includes: centrally argues for national regulatory autonomy over AI; analyzes sovereignty tensions with transnational AI firms; proposes sovereign AI strategies or national AI acts.

Non-interference principle in AI governance, respecting states' internal regulatory autonomy. Strong coverage includes: invokes non-interference principle to resist external AI governance imposition; analyzes tensions between international standards and domestic regulatory autonomy.

AI governance oriented toward peace, civilian protection, and conflict prevention. Strong coverage includes: analyzes AI's role in peace processes, civilian protection, or conflict escalation; proposes AI governance to prevent civilian harm in conflict zones.

The extent to which the statement asserts the right to set autonomous AI regulatory frameworks versus accepting imported standards. Strong coverage includes: explicitly asserts regulatory sovereignty; resists harmonization pressures from dominant AI powers; proposes nationally or regionally tailored regulatory frameworks independent of EU/US models.

Abrahamic stewardship and trusteeship: humans as stewards (khilafa) or created in God's image (imago Dei), bearing responsibility for creation including technology. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes khilafa or imago Dei; argues human stewardship responsibility extends to AI; proposes theological grounding for AI governance obligations.

Ahimsa (non-harm) from Dharmic traditions (Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism) applied to AI ethics. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes ahimsa; applies non-harm across all sentient beings to AI; proposes AI design principles grounded in Dharmic non-violence.

Ethics of care applied to AI: relational ethics, responsiveness to vulnerability, and caring relationships. Strong coverage includes: applies care ethics framework; analyzes AI through lens of caring relationships, attentiveness, responsibility, and responsiveness; critiques abstract justice approaches.

Kantian deontological principles: treating persons as ends, universalizability, moral autonomy. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Kant; applies categorical imperative, humanity formula, or universalizability test to AI; argues AI must never reduce persons to mere means.

Catholic Social Teaching applied to AI: subsidiarity, solidarity, preferential option for the poor, and common good. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes CST principles (subsidiarity, solidarity, common good); applies papal encyclicals or Vatican AI statements to AI governance; proposes CST-aligned policy.

Context-appropriate information flows: AI systems must respect established informational norms of specific social contexts. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Nissenbaum's contextual integrity; analyzes AI data flows against context-relative norms; proposes CI-based privacy frameworks for AI.

Covenantal justice and restorative justice rooted in Hebrew tzedek: justice as right-relationship, repair, and covenant faithfulness. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes tzedek, covenantal justice, or restorative justice in Abrahamic tradition; proposes repair-centered AI accountability grounded in relational justice theology.

Dharmic order (dharma/karma) applied to AI: cosmic accountability, moral causation, and duty-based action. Strong coverage includes: invokes dharma or karma frameworks; applies moral causation and duty concepts to AI accountability; proposes dharma-aligned AI governance.

Fair distribution of AI's benefits and burdens across society. Strong coverage includes: centrally analyzes distributional impacts of AI; proposes specific redistribution mechanisms (taxation, benefit sharing, access mandates) or equity metrics.

Non-maleficence principle: AI systems should not cause harm; harm prevention takes priority. Strong coverage includes: non-maleficence is a central organizing principle; analyzes harm types, thresholds, and trade-offs; proposes harm prevention mechanisms or red lines.

Testimonial and hermeneutical injustice in AI: systems that systematically discredit certain knowers or lack interpretive resources for marginalized experiences. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Fricker or epistemic injustice; analyzes AI systems as sites of testimonial/hermeneutical injustice; proposes epistemic justice interventions.

Fiduciary or stewardship obligations for AI developers and deployers toward affected persons. Strong coverage includes: proposes information fiduciary or stewardship duties for AI actors; analyzes trust relationships between AI developers and users; argues for legal fiduciary frameworks.

Capabilities approach to AI justice: AI should expand human capabilities and freedoms, not merely increase utility. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Sen or Nussbaum; applies capabilities list to AI assessment; argues AI policy should be evaluated by capability expansion for the least capable.

The extent to which the statement promotes multi-faith collaborative approaches to AI governance. Strong coverage includes: explicitly proposes interfaith collaboration on AI ethics; identifies shared values across religious traditions as basis for AI governance; organizes or references multi-faith AI dialogues or joint statements.

Ethical obligations to future generations in AI development and governance. Strong coverage includes: applies formal intergenerational ethics (Jonas, Parfit, or similar); argues for long-term thinking in AI governance; proposes future-generation representation mechanisms.

Islamic public interest (maslaha), justice (adl), and trusteeship (amanah) applied to AI governance. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes maslaha, adl, or amanah; applies Islamic jurisprudential principles to AI; proposes Shariah-compatible AI governance frameworks.

Islamic prohibition of excessive uncertainty (gharar) applied to AI: requiring transparency and prohibiting deceptive algorithmic opacity. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes gharar prohibition; argues AI opacity violates Islamic principles of transparency in transactions; proposes gharar-based explainability requirements.

Islamic consultative governance (shura) applied to AI: collective deliberation and consensus-seeking. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes shura; proposes consultative AI governance models grounded in Islamic deliberation tradition; analyzes parallels with participatory governance.

Rawlsian justice applied to AI: veil of ignorance, difference principle, fair equality of opportunity. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Rawls; applies veil of ignorance or difference principle to AI design; argues AI benefits must flow to least advantaged.

Acceptance of multiple valid moral frameworks; AI governance must accommodate irreducible value diversity. Strong coverage includes: explicitly argues for moral pluralism in AI; proposes governance mechanisms that accommodate multiple ethical frameworks; critiques single-framework approaches.

Commitment to universal moral principles applicable across all cultures in AI governance. Strong coverage includes: explicitly argues for universal moral principles in AI; defends cross-cultural validity of core norms (dignity, rights); critiques moral relativism in AI ethics.

Precautionary approach to AI risks: where potential harms are serious, lack of certainty should not delay protective action. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes precautionary principle; argues for burden-of-proof reversal on AI developers; proposes moratoriums, mandatory risk assessment, or safety-first deployment rules.

Protection of sacred, ceremonial, and indigenous knowledge systems from AI extraction, digitization, or misappropriation. Strong coverage includes: argues for protections of sacred or indigenous knowledge from AI training data extraction; proposes indigenous data sovereignty protocols or sacred knowledge firewalls.

Sanctity of human life as non-tradeable threshold: AI must never be permitted to devalue or commodify human life. Strong coverage includes: invokes sanctity of life as absolute constraint on AI; argues certain AI applications (lethal autonomous weapons, life-and-death triage) violate inviolable human dignity.

Social contract theory applied to AI: legitimate governance requires consent of the governed. Strong coverage includes: explicitly applies social contract theory to AI governance; argues for consent-based legitimacy, proposes new social contracts for the AI age, or analyzes democratic mandate for AI regulation.

Solidarity as ethical principle in AI governance, emphasizing mutual aid, collective responsibility, and social bonds (European social tradition). Strong coverage includes: invokes solidarity as a foundational principle; proposes collective responsibility frameworks, mutual aid structures, or solidarity-based AI governance mechanisms.

Ubuntu-based communal accountability and restorative justice applied to AI governance. Strong coverage includes: applies Ubuntu accountability frameworks to AI; proposes restorative rather than punitive approaches to AI harms; argues for communal rather than individual liability models.

Ubuntu philosophy: personhood is constituted through relationships; 'I am because we are' applied to AI design. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes Ubuntu; argues AI must support relational personhood; proposes community-centered AI design grounded in African relational ontology.

Utilitarian reasoning: maximizing aggregate welfare or wellbeing through AI. Strong coverage includes: explicitly applies utilitarian calculus to AI governance; analyzes welfare maximization, cost-benefit frameworks, or proposes aggregate wellbeing metrics for AI evaluation.

Virtue ethics applied to AI: cultivating human excellence, practical wisdom, and moral character in AI contexts. Strong coverage includes: applies virtue ethics framework to AI; discusses phronesis, character formation, or how AI supports/undermines human flourishing and moral development.

Special ethical obligations to protect vulnerable persons (children, elderly, disabled, refugees) from AI harms. Strong coverage includes: centers vulnerability as an organizing ethical concept; identifies specific vulnerable populations and AI risks they face; proposes heightened protections or vulnerability-sensitive design.
Audit and certification: ongoing compliance auditing of AI systems

The extent to which the statement addresses tort law adaptation, burden of proof, and compensation frameworks for AI-caused harms. Strong coverage includes: proposes specific civil liability rules for AI harms; addresses burden of proof allocation, strict vs. fault-based liability, or compensation mechanisms; references product liability adaptation for AI.

Extent to which the statement proposes a co-regulatory approach combining government authority with industry participation. Strong coverage includes: defines a co-regulatory structure where government sets goals and industry develops implementation standards; specifies roles, accountability, and oversight mechanisms.

The extent to which the statement anchors AI governance in constitutional rights, principles, or frameworks. Strong coverage includes: explicitly grounds AI governance in constitutional provisions; invokes constitutional rights (dignity, equality, privacy) as binding constraints on AI; proposes constitutional amendments or interpretations for AI.
Extraterritorial application: rules applying beyond national borders
Foundation model governance: specific rules for large base models
General-purpose AI rules: horizontal regulation for foundation models

The extent to which the statement proposes institutional capability for monitoring emerging AI developments and anticipating future risks. Strong coverage includes: proposes dedicated horizon scanning bodies, technology assessment offices, or foresight units for AI; mandates periodic technology outlook reports; establishes early warning systems for emerging AI risks.
Incident reporting: mandatory reporting of AI failures or harms
International harmonization: aligning AI rules across jurisdictions
Interoperability standards: technical standards enabling AI compatibility
Liability allocation: who bears legal responsibility for AI harms

The extent to which the statement addresses governance requirements across the full AI lifecycle from design through deployment to decommission. Strong coverage includes: proposes governance requirements spanning the full AI lifecycle; addresses design-phase obligations, deployment monitoring, and decommission/retirement procedures; mandates lifecycle documentation.

The extent to which the statement proposes compulsory insurance schemes for AI operators or deployers. Strong coverage includes: explicitly proposes mandatory insurance for AI systems; specifies coverage requirements, risk pools, or insurance obligations for high-risk AI applications.
Mandatory impact assessments: DPIA, algorithmic impact assessments required
Market surveillance: post-market monitoring by regulators
Mutual recognition: accepting other countries' regulatory approvals
Open source AI treatment: how open-weight models are regulated

Extent to which the statement mandates ongoing monitoring of AI systems after deployment. Strong coverage includes: specifies post-market surveillance obligations; defines monitoring metrics, reporting frequency, and triggers for system withdrawal or modification.
Pre-deployment approval: regulatory sign-off before release

The extent to which the statement establishes that individuals can bring legal action against AI harms. Strong coverage includes: explicitly establishes private right of action for AI harms; specifies standing requirements, available remedies, or procedural rules for individual lawsuits against AI operators.
Public registers: databases of AI systems, transparency registers
Redress mechanisms: pathways for victims of AI harm to seek compensation

The extent to which the statement expresses concern that AI regulators may be captured by the industry they regulate. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses regulatory capture risks; proposes structural safeguards (revolving door restrictions, conflict of interest rules, independent funding); analyzes industry influence on AI regulation.
Risk classification: tiered approach to AI risk (high/medium/low risk categories)
Safe harbor provisions: liability protection under certain conditions
Regulatory sandboxes: safe experimentation spaces with relaxed rules
Sector-specific AI rules: tailored regulation by industry sector
Self-declaration: companies attest their own compliance

The extent to which the statement addresses capital-market mechanisms for AI accountability, including shareholder activism and investor oversight. Strong coverage includes: proposes shareholder or investor-driven AI governance mechanisms; addresses AI risk disclosure requirements for investors, ESG integration of AI risks, or fiduciary duty extensions for AI oversight.
Supply chain accountability: upstream/downstream responsibility
Third-party oversight: external auditors, independent assessors
Voluntary commitments: industry pledges, non-binding agreements

Extent to which the statement provides protections for individuals reporting AI-related misconduct or harms. Strong coverage includes: establishes specific whistleblower protections for AI contexts; defines protected disclosures, anti-retaliation measures, and reporting channels.
Affected community input: consulting those impacted by AI systems
Algorithmic auditing: systematic examination of AI system behavior
Bias testing and evaluation: measuring and mitigating discrimination
Capability evaluation: assessing what AI systems can and cannot do
Compute governance: controlling access to AI training resources
Dataset documentation: datasheets, data cards, provenance records
Differential privacy: mathematical privacy guarantees in AI
Evidence-based policy: grounding AI rules in empirical evidence
Explainable AI (XAI): methods for making AI decisions understandable
Federated learning: training AI without centralizing data
Human-in-the-loop design: systems requiring human involvement
Technical interpretability: research into understanding model internals
Model cards: standardized documentation of AI model capabilities and limitations
Open data standards: shared, accessible data for AI training and evaluation
Open weights access: public access to AI model parameters
Participatory design: including affected users in AI system design
Pre-registration: committing to research design before data collection
Privacy-preserving AI techniques: protecting data while enabling AI
Public consultation: broad public input into AI policy
Red teaming: adversarial testing for vulnerabilities and failure modes
Reproducibility: ability to replicate AI research and results
Safety benchmarking: standardized tests for safety properties
Scientific peer review: independent scientific scrutiny of AI claims
Secure multiparty computation: collaborative AI without data exposure
Synthetic data: AI-generated data for training and testing
System cards: documentation of complete AI system context and use
Uncertainty quantification: AI knowing and communicating what it doesn't know
Watermarking and provenance: tracking AI-generated content origins
Academic research freedom: independence of AI research
Affected community standing: formal role for impacted groups
Big Tech accountability: obligations of major AI companies
Child protection online: safeguards for minors in AI contexts
Civil society role: NGOs, advocates in AI governance
Consumer protection: rights of AI consumers
Corporate power concentration: concerns about Big Tech control
Deployer responsibilities: obligations of those who deploy AI
Developer responsibilities: obligations of AI creators
Developing country voice: Global South in AI governance
Elderly and vulnerable protection: special AI safeguards
Government role in AI: state responsibilities and powers
Indigenous data sovereignty: First Nations control over AI and data
Labor voice: worker representation in AI governance
Media role: journalism, watchdog function in AI accountability
Multistakeholder governance: inclusive AI governance model
Platform liability: legal responsibility of AI/tech platforms
Professional ethics codes: domain-specific AI ethics for practitioners

The extent to which the statement addresses how specific professions or sectors must adapt to AI integration. Strong coverage includes: centrally addresses sector-specific AI adaptation; proposes profession-specific guidelines, training requirements, or practice standards for AI use in specific fields (medicine, law, education, engineering).
Regulator independence: autonomy of AI oversight bodies from industry
Researcher responsibility: ethical obligations of AI scientists
Small state capacity: AI governance for resource-constrained countries
User rights: entitlements of people using AI systems
Autonomous weapons risks: lethal autonomous systems
Bioweapons risks: AI accelerating biological weapons development
Catastrophic risks: AI causing large-scale civilizational harm
Children exploitation risks: AI used to exploit minors
Concentration of power risks: AI enabling authoritarianism or monopoly
Critical infrastructure risks: AI attacks on power, water, finance
Cultural erasure risks: AI homogenizing or erasing cultures
Cyberattack risks: AI-enhanced offensive cyber capabilities
Democratic erosion risks: AI undermining democratic institutions
Discrimination and bias harms: unfair treatment by AI systems
Dual-use and weapons risks: AI repurposed for weapons
Environmental harms: AI's ecological damage
Epistemic harms: AI damaging the quality of knowledge and discourse
Existential risks: AI posing threat to human survival
Financial harms: AI causing economic losses to individuals
Gender-based harms: AI disproportionately harming women/gender minorities
Labor displacement harms: job losses from AI automation
Manipulation and persuasion harms: AI exploiting psychology
Minority community harms: AI causing disproportionate harm to minorities
Misinformation and deepfake harms: AI-generated false content
Physical safety harms: AI causing bodily harm
Privacy and surveillance harms: AI-enabled violations of privacy
Psychological harms: AI causing mental health damage
Surveillance state risks: AI-enabled authoritarian control
Systemic risks: AI causing cascading failures in critical systems
AI alignment research: technical work on aligning AI goals with human values
Capability control: limiting what AI systems can do
Constitutional AI: rule-based constraints on AI behavior
Containment and sandboxing: isolating AI systems from real-world impact
Deployment gating: staged rollout with safety checks
Fail-safe design: defaulting to safe behavior on failure
Formal verification: mathematical proofs of AI system properties
Goal stability: ensuring AI pursues intended objectives over time
Human override: ability to supersede AI decisions
Incident response: procedures for AI safety incidents
Interpretability for safety: understanding models to prevent harms
Safety-focused model evaluation: benchmarks for safety properties
Monitoring and anomaly detection: runtime safety monitoring
Red teaming for safety: adversarial testing for safety failures
Reward hacking prevention: stopping AI from gaming evaluation metrics
Adversarial robustness: resistance to attacks and distribution shift
Scalable oversight: supervising AI that exceeds human capabilities
Shutdown mechanisms: ability to safely stop AI systems
Transparency logging: audit trails of AI system actions
Value learning: AI learning human preferences and values
Communitarian framing: community and collective values
Cosmopolitan framing: global citizenship and universal values
Decolonial framing: AI through postcolonial lens
Economic competitiveness framing: AI race dynamics
Feminist framing: AI through gender equality lens
Governance-first framing: institutional capacity before deployment
Human flourishing framing: AI for authentic human wellbeing
Innovation-first framing: prioritizing growth over precaution
Libertarian framing: minimal regulation, individual freedom
National security framing: AI through defense and security lens
Precautionary framing: better safe than sorry approach to AI
Religious and moral framing: theological or traditional ethics
Rights-based framing: AI through human rights lens
Risk-based framing: proportionate response to AI risk levels
Safety-first framing: prioritizing harm prevention over innovation
Solidarity framing: collective responsibility and mutual aid
Techno-optimism: positive framing of AI potential and progress
Techno-scepticism: critical framing of AI claims and risks
Utilitarian framing: greatest good for greatest number
Virtue ethics framing: character and excellence of AI actors
Bilateral cooperation: specific country-to-country AI agreements
Capacity building emphasis: building AI governance institutions globally
Chinese model alignment: reflects Chinese governance approach to AI
Competitive AI race framing: AI as geopolitical competition
Cooperative AI development: collaborative international approach
EU Brussels Effect: explicitly adopts or advocates EU regulatory approach
Global South advocacy: centered on developing country interests
Multilateral UN system: engagement with UN AI governance
Non-aligned positioning: explicitly avoiding bloc alignment
Open internet advocacy: free flow of AI across borders
Regional bloc approach: ASEAN, AU, etc. regional coordination
Sanctions and export controls: restricting AI technology flows
Splinternet acceptance: acceptance of fragmented internet/AI governance
Technology transfer advocacy: sharing AI with developing nations
US market-led approach: light-touch regulation, industry self-governance
Western liberal alignment: document reflects Western liberal democratic norms
Appeal procedures: formal process for challenging decisions
Aspirational only: vague principles without concrete commitments
Cross-sector coordination: linking AI to other policy domains
Definitions provided: clear definitions of key terms including 'AI'
Enforcement mechanisms: penalties, sanctions, consequences
Evidence base cited: references to empirical research
Exceptions and carve-outs: explicit exclusions from requirements
Hard law tools: regulations, statutes, binding rules
High specificity: concrete, detailed, actionable provisions
Implementation roadmap: clear steps and milestones
Legislative hooks: references to or calls for legislation
Monitoring framework: tracking progress and outcomes
Pilot programs: experimental or phased implementation
Resource commitments: funding, staffing, infrastructure
Review cycle: scheduled reassessment of the document
Scope limitations stated: explicit statement of what is covered
Soft law tools: guidelines, codes, voluntary commitments
Stakeholder consultation: evidence of inclusive process
Sunset clauses: automatic expiration provisions
Timeline commitments: specific dates and deadlines

The extent to which the statement mandates legally binding worker participation in AI deployment decisions. Strong coverage includes: explicitly mandates worker co-determination in AI decisions; proposes works council consultation rights, collective bargaining over AI, or legally binding worker veto on workplace AI deployments.
Criminal justice sector: AI in policing, courts, corrections
Critical infrastructure: AI in power grids, water, communications
Defense and security sector: military AI, national security applications
Education sector: AI in teaching, assessment, learning
Employment and HR sector: AI in hiring, performance management
Financial services sector: AI in banking, insurance, credit

The extent to which the statement addresses the risk of AI safety measures being bypassed by end users. Strong coverage includes: explicitly addresses guardrail circumvention risks (jailbreaking, prompt injection, safety bypass techniques); proposes robust safety mechanisms resistant to user circumvention; mandates red-teaming for circumvention resilience.
Healthcare sector: AI in medical diagnosis, treatment, healthcare delivery
Media and entertainment sector: AI in content creation, distribution
Public administration sector: AI in government services
Scientific research: AI in academic and R&D contexts
Social services: AI in welfare, benefits, social care
Transport and autonomous vehicles: self-driving, logistics AI
AI education and training: formal curriculum, workforce skills

The extent to which the statement expresses aspiration to become a regional or global AI center of excellence. Strong coverage includes: explicitly articulates AI hub aspiration; proposes strategies to attract AI investment, talent, or research institutions; frames the jurisdiction as a future AI center or innovation hub.
Civil society capacity: building NGO ability to engage AI governance

The extent to which the statement frames AI adoption as an opportunity to skip traditional development stages. Strong coverage includes: explicitly invokes digital leapfrogging; frames AI as enabling countries to bypass intermediate development stages; proposes AI-first strategies for healthcare, education, or infrastructure in developing contexts.
Digital literacy emphasis: training public to understand AI
Government capacity: building state ability to govern AI
International capacity gap: addressing global inequality in AI governance ability
Private sector self-regulation: industry-led standards and codes
Public AI investment: government funding for AI development
Public trust building: initiatives to increase AI confidence
Regulatory expertise: specialized technical capacity for regulators
Research infrastructure: computing, data, facilities for AI research

The extent to which the statement envisions government providing AI infrastructure, data platforms, or computational resources for private sector use. Strong coverage includes: explicitly proposes state-as-platform model; government provides AI infrastructure (compute, data, APIs) for private sector and civil society use; frames public AI infrastructure as public good.
Testbed and sandbox infrastructure: facilities for safe AI experimentation
