concepts.label: Transparency
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems, their purposes, capabilities, and limitations should be understandable and open to scrutiny.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: explainability, data disclosure, organizational openness. Excludes: open-source licensing (see open_source_ai).

children.label: Algorithmic Explainability
children.definition: Methods and requirements for making AI decision-making processes understandable to humans.

children.label: Data Transparency
children.definition: Requirements for disclosure of data used in AI training and operation.

children.label: Organizational Transparency
children.definition: Requirements for organizational openness about AI development and deployment practices.

concepts.label: Accountability
concepts.definition: The principle that identifiable actors bear responsibility for AI system outcomes and that mechanisms exist for redress.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: liability, contestability, due diligence. Excludes: financial accounting.

children.label: Liability
children.definition: Legal liability frameworks for AI system developers, deployers, and operators.

children.label: Redress Mechanisms
children.definition: Mechanisms enabling individuals harmed by AI to seek and obtain remedy.

children.label: Contestability
children.definition: The ability for individuals to challenge or dispute AI-driven decisions.

children.label: Responsible AI
children.definition: Frameworks requiring organizations to take responsibility for ethical AI development and deployment.

concepts.label: Fairness
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems should not discriminate and should provide equitable access and outcomes.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: non-discrimination, equitable access, bias mitigation. Excludes: fair market competition (see competition_market).

children.label: Non-Discrimination
children.definition: Requirements that AI systems not discriminate against individuals or groups.

children.label: Equitable Access
children.definition: Ensuring fair and equal access to AI technologies and their benefits.

children.label: Representation
children.definition: Ensuring diverse representation in AI development teams and affected communities.

children.label: Bias Mitigation
children.definition: Techniques and requirements for detecting and reducing bias in AI systems.

concepts.label: Privacy
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems should protect personal data and respect informational self-determination.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: data protection, surveillance, consent. Excludes: trade secrets.

children.label: Data Protection
children.definition: Legal and technical measures protecting personal data in AI contexts.

children.label: Surveillance Concerns
children.definition: Concerns about AI-enabled surveillance and its impact on privacy.

children.label: Consent Mechanisms
children.definition: Mechanisms for obtaining meaningful consent for AI data processing.

concepts.label: Safety
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems should be safe, robust, and not cause harm to individuals or society.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: harm prevention, robustness, safety engineering. Excludes: cybersecurity (except as it affects safety).

children.label: Harm Prevention
children.definition: Measures to prevent AI systems from causing harm to individuals or society.

children.label: Robustness & Reliability
children.definition: Requirements for AI systems to function reliably and resist perturbation.

children.label: Safety by Design
children.definition: Integrating safety considerations into AI system design from the outset.

concepts.label: Human Oversight
concepts.definition: The principle that humans should maintain meaningful control over AI systems and their decisions.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: human-in-the-loop, operator oversight, human agency. Excludes: automated monitoring (see post_market_monitoring).

children.label: HITL System Design
children.definition: Design patterns ensuring meaningful human involvement in AI system operation.

children.label: Operator Oversight
children.definition: Requirements for human operators to oversee AI system operation.

children.label: Human Agency & Autonomy
children.definition: Preserving human agency and autonomy in interactions with AI systems.

concepts.label: Beneficence
concepts.definition: The principle that AI should promote human wellbeing and contribute positively to society.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: human flourishing, societal benefit, common good. Excludes: commercial benefit.

children.label: Human Flourishing
children.definition: AI systems should contribute to human flourishing and wellbeing.

children.label: Societal Benefit
children.definition: AI should generate broad societal benefit rather than narrow interests.

children.label: Common Good
children.definition: AI governance should serve the common good across generations.

concepts.label: Sustainability
concepts.definition: The principle that AI development and deployment should be environmentally sustainable.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: environmental impact, SDG alignment. Excludes: financial sustainability.

children.label: Environmental Impact of AI
children.definition: The environmental consequences of AI development and deployment.

children.label: Sustainable Development Goals
children.definition: Aligning AI with sustainable development goals and frameworks.

concepts.label: Dignity
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems should respect the inherent worth and dignity of every human being.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: inherent worth, dehumanization risks. Excludes: dignity as social status.

children.label: Imago Dei / Sacred Personhood
children.definition: The theological concept of humans as created in the image of God, grounding inherent dignity.

children.label: AI Personhood Concern
children.definition: Concerns about attributing personhood or moral status to AI systems.

concepts.label: Autonomy
concepts.definition: The principle that AI should respect and preserve human autonomy and self-determination.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: individual choice, cognitive autonomy, democratic participation. Excludes: AI system autonomy (see ai_autonomy).

children.label: Individual Autonomy
children.definition: Protecting individual self-determination in AI-mediated decisions.

children.label: Collective / Community Autonomy
children.definition: Protecting community and democratic self-governance from AI disruption.

concepts.label: Solidarity
concepts.definition: The principle that AI governance should promote social cohesion and global cooperation.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: community resilience, global solidarity. Excludes: political solidarity movements.

children.label: Social Cohesion
children.definition: Maintaining social bonds and community resilience in the face of AI change.

children.label: Global Solidarity
children.definition: International cooperation and equity in AI governance.

concepts.label: Justice
concepts.definition: The principle that AI systems should promote just outcomes and fair processes.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: distributive justice, procedural justice. Excludes: criminal justice system (except as AI-impacted).

children.label: Distributive Justice
children.definition: Fair distribution of AI benefits and burdens across society.

children.label: Procedural Justice
children.definition: Fair processes in AI governance and decision-making.

concepts.label: Trust
concepts.definition: The warranted confidence that AI systems, their developers, and deployers will act reliably, safely, and in accordance with stated values and commitments.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: trustworthiness requirements, trust-building mechanisms, public confidence. Excludes: trust as interpersonal social bond (see solidarity).

children.label: Trustworthy AI
children.definition: Frameworks and requirements for AI systems to be deemed trustworthy.

children.label: Public Trust
children.definition: Public and societal confidence in AI systems and governance.

concepts.label: Regulatory Approach
concepts.definition: The spectrum of governance instruments available for regulating AI systems.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: risk-based, rights-based, self-regulation, prescriptive, principles-based approaches. Excludes: informal norms.

children.label: Risk-Based Regulation
children.definition: Regulation calibrated to the level of risk posed by AI systems.

children.label: Rights-Based Regulation
children.definition: Regulation grounded in fundamental rights protections.

children.label: Self-Regulation
children.definition: Industry-led governance through codes of conduct and voluntary commitments.

children.label: Prescriptive / Command-and-Control
children.definition: Command-and-control regulation specifying prohibited or required behaviors.

children.label: Principles-Based Regulation
children.definition: Regulation structured around high-level principles rather than detailed rules.

children.label: Generative AI Governance
children.definition: Governance frameworks specifically addressing generative AI systems, foundation models, and general-purpose AI.

children.label: Prohibited Practices
children.definition: AI applications explicitly prohibited or severely restricted by regulation due to unacceptable risks to fundamental rights.

children.label: Precautionary Principle
children.definition: Positions advocating caution and precaution in AI development.

concepts.label: Institutional Design
concepts.definition: The organizational structures and bodies responsible for AI governance.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: oversight bodies, ethics boards, multi-stakeholder governance. Excludes: corporate organizational structure.

children.label: AI Oversight Body
children.definition: Dedicated governmental bodies responsible for AI governance and supervision.

children.label: Ethics Board / Committee
children.definition: Advisory or oversight bodies focused on ethical dimensions of AI.

children.label: Multi-Stakeholder Governance
children.definition: Governance models involving multiple societal actors.

concepts.label: Technical Standard
concepts.definition: Technical standards and assessment frameworks for AI systems.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: impact assessment, certification, interoperability standards. Excludes: industry product standards.

children.label: Algorithmic Impact Assessment
children.definition: Structured assessment of the impact of AI systems on individuals and society.

children.label: Certification & Audit
children.definition: Independent verification and certification of AI system compliance.

children.label: Interoperability Standard
children.definition: Technical standards enabling AI system interoperability.

concepts.label: Enforcement Mechanism
concepts.definition: Mechanisms for ensuring compliance with AI governance rules.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: penalties, monitoring, whistleblower protection. Excludes: self-enforcement through design.

children.label: Penalty / Sanction
children.definition: Penalties and sanctions for non-compliance with AI governance rules.

children.label: Monitoring & Reporting
children.definition: Ongoing monitoring and incident reporting requirements for AI systems.

children.label: Whistleblower Rights
children.definition: Protections for individuals reporting AI-related misconduct or violations.

concepts.label: Innovation Posture
concepts.definition: Positions on balancing AI innovation with governance and precaution.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: pro-innovation, responsible innovation. Excludes: precautionary principle (now under regulatory_approach).

children.label: Innovation-First / Pro-Development
children.definition: Positions prioritizing innovation speed and competitive advantage.

children.label: Responsible Innovation
children.definition: Positions advocating innovation balanced with ethical guardrails.

concepts.label: Sovereignty & Self-Determination
concepts.definition: Positions on national, cultural, and indigenous sovereignty in AI governance.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: data sovereignty, cultural sovereignty, technological sovereignty. Excludes: state sovereignty in international law.

children.label: Data Flows & Sovereignty
children.definition: Governance of cross-border data flows and data localization requirements.

children.label: Cultural / Epistemic Sovereignty
children.definition: Protecting cultural values and epistemic traditions from AI homogenization.

children.label: Technological Sovereignty
children.definition: National or regional capacity for independent AI development.

children.label: Indigenous Data Governance
children.definition: Indigenous community governance over their data and knowledge.

concepts.label: Human-AI Relationship
concepts.definition: Framings of how humans should relate to AI systems.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: AI as tool, AI as agent, spiritual/theological framings. Excludes: human-computer interaction research.

children.label: AI as Tool
children.definition: Framing AI as a tool serving human purposes.

children.label: AI as Agent / Partner
children.definition: Framing AI as an autonomous agent or partner.

children.label: Spiritual / Theological Framing
children.definition: Religious and spiritual perspectives on AI and technology.

concepts.label: Economic Position
concepts.definition: Positions on the economic implications of AI.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: worker protection, competition, development capacity. Excludes: macroeconomic modeling.

children.label: Worker Protection / Just Transition
children.definition: Protecting workers from negative impacts of AI automation.

children.label: Competition & Market Access
children.definition: Competition law and market access considerations for AI.

children.label: Development & Capacity Building
children.definition: Building capacity for AI development and adoption.

concepts.label: Geopolitical Position
concepts.definition: Positions on AI in international relations and geopolitics.
concepts.scope_note: Includes: AI arms race, Global South inclusion, international cooperation. Excludes: general foreign policy.

children.label: AI Arms Race Concern
children.definition: Concerns about competitive AI development in military contexts.

children.label: Digital Colonialism
children.definition: The risk of AI perpetuating colonial power dynamics in the digital sphere.

children.label: International Cooperation
children.definition: International frameworks for AI governance cooperation.
